title: Reversibility of Ischemic Brain Damage
abstract:
Reversibility of
Ischemic Brain Damage
Konstantin-A. Hossmann, MD, Paul Kleihues, MD,
Cologne, West Germany
The
susceptibility of the brain to
anoxia is considered to be the
limiting factor for resuscitation after
transient circulatory arrest, both in
clinical
and
experimental
condi-
tions.1,2 In the classic experiments of
Weinberger et al,3 Kabat et al,4 Gre-
nell,5 Hirsch et al,6 and others, the
upper limit for full
recovery
was
found to be three to four minutes of
cardiocirculatory arrest, and eight to
ten minutes of isolated cerebrovascu-
lar arrest.
The high sensitivity of the central
nervous tissue to ischemia has been
attributed to low
reserves in sub-
strates suitable for anaerobic energy
metabolism, because a close correla-
tion was found between the depletion
of energy-rich phosphates and the
irreversibility of brain damage.7 This
concept, however, is difficult to recon-
cile with more recent demonstrations
of the recovery of various neuronal
functions after time intervals which
by far exceed the limit of energy de-
pletion. Examples are the return of
evoked potentials,8 the electroretin-
ogram,9 the pyramidal response to
the electrical stimulation of the mo¬
tor cortex,1" and the reappearance of
spontaneous electroencephalographic
activity after one hour of complete
ischemia at normothermia." Recov-
ery of these functions, which depend
on
the availability of energy
re¬
sources, indicate that nerve cells may
survive extended periods of ischemia
under suitable conditions. Ischemie
brain damage as observed in earlier
experiments, therefore, is likely to be
not merely the consequence of an
inherent susceptibility of the nerve
cell to anoxia, but a complex patho¬
genic process in which extraneural
factors are involved. One of these fac¬
tors is the no-reflow phenomenon,12
ie, the impairment of postischemic
blood recirculation; other factors may
be related to the composition of blood
and the cerebrospinal fluid (CSF). A
study of these complicating side ef¬
fects is particularly interesting be¬
cause avoiding them might lengthen
the period of ischemia after which
the brain can be resuscitated. This
paper summarizes the investigations
that have been carried out in our
laboratory10·11·13-23
Material and Methods
Production of Ischemia.
— More than
200 cats and 21 monkeys (Macacca mulat¬
ta) were used. The animals were anesthe¬
tized with pentobarbital (Nembutal, 30
mg/kg), immobilized with gallamine trie-
thiodide (Flaxedil), and artificially venti¬
lated with
room air. Cerebral ischemia
was produced at normothermia by intra-
thoracal clamping of the innominate and
left subclavian arteries and lowering of
the blood pressure to less than 80 mm Hg
after previous ligation of the internal
mammary arteries in order to prevent a
collateral blood supply to the brain. At the
end of the ischemie period, the blood pres¬
sure was raised with sympathomimetics
and kept as high as possible for as long as
possible. Cardiac insufficiency was treated
with glycosides; derangements in gas pres¬
sures, acid-base balance, and electrolyte
contents of the blood were reversed by
appropriate infusions of buffers, salt solu¬
tions, and adjustments of the respiration
pump. Details concerning the operative
procedure, physiologic recordings, and the
investigation
of
brain
material
after
ischemia
have
been
published
else¬
where.  »-1'·'«'»
Evidence for Completeness of Ische¬
mia.—With the methods employed, ische¬
mia was complete in 85% of the experi¬
ments. This was confirmed by dye injec¬
tion, microscopic observations of the pial
vessels, absence of entrance of labeled
substances into the brain, absence of in¬
tracranial bleeding after removal of the
brain, and absence of clearance of radioac¬
tive tracers injected into the brain after
the onset of ischemia.
Observation of the brain surface with an
operation microscope revealed that arteri¬
al clamping caused an immediate arrest of
the blood flow in all pial vessels. After an
interval of a few seconds, a slow pulsatile
outflow of the blood into the larger veins
became apparent, which was accompanied
by a continuous reduction in brain vol¬
ume. After three to five minutes, most of
the blood vessels were completely empty
and pulse synchronous pulsations ceased;
but slight respiratory movements, which
were probably transferred by
pressure
changes in the CSF, remained visible
throughout the ischemie period.
The ischemie regions could be clearly
delimited by the absence of staining after
intravenous injection of Evans blue, and
comprised in both species of animals the
total head inclusive of brain, the forepaws,
and the upper part of the trunk and spinal
cord up to approximately T-5.
Quantitative blood flow measurements
in those animals in which ischemia was
not complete revealed a remaining flow of

 less than 10% of the normal.15 Ischemie
suppression of electrophysiologic activity
was slightly delayed, but functional
re¬
covery was usually worse than in animals
with complete ischemia (see below).
Brain Temperature During
Ische¬
mia.—In the cat series brain temperature
fell slowly during ischemia. Temperature
drop
1
mm
under the
surface of the
cortex"1 was about 0.2 C/min, and room air
temperature (24 to 28 C) was reached not
earlier than 60 minutes after the begin¬
ning of ischemia.
In the monkey series brain temperature
was kept constant by a bath of warm min¬
eral oil. In spite of the higher tempera¬
ture, the results were not inferior to those
in cats, indicating that the temperature
drop during ischemia was of minor impor¬
tance.
Results
Our method of producing cerebral
ischemia
was
not appropriate for
long-term
experiments.
The
main
objective, therefore,
was
to define
postischemic
recovery in
terms of
basic neurophysiologic and biochemi¬
cal functions and to evaluate those
factors that might interfere with the
recovery process.
Unless otherwise
stated, our observations were made
after complete cerebral ischemia of 60
minutes in normothermic cats and
monkeys.
Electrophysiologic Observations
and Definitions of Functional Re¬
covery.
— Several
neurophysiologic
measurements
were used to
assess
the functional state of the brain dur¬
ing and after ischemia14·19 (Fig 1).
Changes in electrical excitability of
the neurons were monitored by
re¬
cording the D wave of pyramidal re¬
sponse to the electrical stimulation of
the motor cortex. Synaptic transmis¬
sion was evidenced by the I wave of
the pyramidal response, and
more
complex cortical functions were
as¬
sayed
by
recording
the
primary
evoked potentials and the electroen¬
cephalogram (EEG) (Fig 1). During
ischemia, the EEG became isoelectric
within 20 seconds; the evoked poten¬
tials and the I wave of the pyramidal
response disappeared after two to
four minutes. The suppression of the I
wave corresponded "with the initial
increase in cortical impedance and
suppression of the D wave with termi¬
nal depolarization
of
the
cortical
steady potential14 (Fig 2). Cortical pH
decreased at a rate of approximately
0.03 units/min and reached values
below 6.8 after 20 minutes. Recovery
of the suppressed functions depended
on the species, the duration of ische¬
mia, the complexity of the functions,
and circulatory factors, which will be
discussed later. After complete ische¬
mia of 60 minutes in normothermic
cats, the shortest latency for recovery
of the D wave of the pyramidal re¬
sponse was 6 minutes, of the I waves
20 minutes, and of the EEG 45 min¬
utes. In the monkey series the corre¬
sponding latencies were 4 minutes for
the D wave, 30 minutes for the I
waves, and three hours for the EEG.
Full recovery required considera¬
bly longer times and could last
as
long as 24 hours. The EEG was pre¬
ceded by a normalization of the corti¬
cal steady potential, cortical imped¬
ance,14 and cortical pH and coincided
with the return of spontaneous activ¬
ity in single cells (Fig 2).
The return of evoked potentials
was different with different modali¬
ties of stimulation.The visual poten¬
tial returned prior to the reappear¬
ance of the EEG and in many cases
went through a phase of hyperexcita-
bility in which the amplitudes were
higher than
before
ischemia.
The
somatically
evoked
potential
re¬
turned together with the first signs of
electroencephalographic activity and
reached control amplitudes not earlier
than six to eight hours after ische¬
mia. In 86 consecutive experiments
with ischemia of 60 minutes, the fol¬
lowing
classification
of functional
recovery was followed on the basis of
electrophysiologic
recordings:
opti¬
mal recovery (13 cats, 3 monkeys)
—
return
of the pyramidal
response
within ten minutes, return of sponta¬
neous EEG waves within 1.5 hours in
cats and within 3 hours in monkeys,
and return of continuous background
activity within 6 hours; fair recovery
(21 cats, 3 monkeys)
— return of the
pyramidal response within 15 min¬
utes,
return
of spontaneous
EEG
waves within 3 hours in cats and 5
hours in monkeys, and no continuous
background activity; bad recovery (21
cats, 3 monkeys)
— partial recovery of
the pyramidal response, and no spon-
taneous EEG
waves;
no persistent
recovery (15 cats, 4 monkeys) —tran¬
sitory recovery of the pyramidal re¬
sponse or no recovery at all.
In the following, the first two groups
are also referred to
as those with
signs of functional recovery, and the
latter two as those without recovery.
Factors Influencing Functional
Recovery
After
Ischemia.— The
occurrence of circulatory disorders
after prolonged ischemia12 suggested
that postischemic blood flow might
play a role in neuronal recovery. In
42 cats and 19 monkeys, quantitative
measurements of cerebral blood flow
were performed by
xenon Xe
133
clearance.11·15 Evaluation of extracer-
ebral flow was done simultaneously
by measuring the flow in the innomi¬
nate artery with magnetic flowme-
ters. In the animals with signs of
functional recovery, ischemia was fol¬
lowed by a period of hyperemia (Fig
3). In cats the average increase in
blood flow after one hour of ischemia
was 80% and after 30 minutes 120%.
In monkeys, hyperemia
after
one
hour of ischemia averaged only 15%.
In cats (but not in monkeys) a clear
correlation existed between the end of
hyperemia and the first reappearance
of spontaneous EEG waves (Fig 3). At
that time autoregulation to changes
in blood pressure was present, but
carbon dioxide reactivity
was still
completely
abolished. Angiography
revealed a diffuse narrowing of the
carotid and internal maxillary
ar¬
teries, but
spasms by
which this
phenomenon could be explained did
not occur. The dissociation between
maintained autoregulation and dis¬
turbed carbon dioxide responsiveness
led to a decrease in the cerebral blood
flow which in some animals obviously
impaired the progression of recovery.
Therapeutic efforts for improvement
with vasoactive drugs were ineffec¬
tive or even harmful because they
frequently
increased
extracerebral
flow at the cost of intracerebral circu¬
lation.15
In animals that did not recover,
blood flow was considerably reduced
after ischemia, and a phase ofhypere¬
mia did not occur. In some cases even
a secondary arrest of blood flow was
observed after a few hours of recircu-

Incompleteness of ischemia seemed
to reduce the changes for functional
recovery. Ischemia was not complete
in nine out of 45 animals that did
not recover, but in only two out of 42
electroencephalographic activity
turned. The tentative explanation is
that in incomplete ischemia the ves¬
sels were filled with slowly moving
blood, the particles of which formed
quate blood recirculation. In complete
ischemia, on the other hand, most
vessels were devoid of their blood con¬
tents which reduced the risk of a mi-

increased. Any recovery of neuronal

metabolism. We have, therefore, de¬

experiments we investigated whether
and to what extent macromolecular
synthesis is resumed after prolonged
cerebral ischemia. By the production
of enzymes, protein synthesis plays a
central role in the regulation of cell
metabolism and is a prerequisite for a
persistent functional recovery. In an
earlier study we determined the in¬
corporation of 14C-labeled amino acids
into cat brain proteins following a 30
minute period of complete ischemia.21
In animals in which recirculation was
accomplished successfully, the specif¬
ic radioactivity of total brain proteins
four hours. At the same time the spe¬

was two to three times higher than in
ferences in the precursor pool were

ent that after three or four hours of
recirculation, the rate of amino acid
incorporation was not more than 30%
to 40% of control animals. Since these

total brain proteins, the possibility
could not be ruled out that the re¬
duced rate of postischemic amino acid
incorporation reflected the fact that
only a minor fraction of cells had sur¬
vived the ischemie impact. To investi¬

somes and monosomes during ische¬
mia similar to that in control ani¬
mals, but a marked disaggregation of
polyribosomes within the first hour of
recirculation.13·21 The breakdown of
polyribosomes resulted in a concomi¬
tant increase in ribosomal subunits,
which suggests an inhibition during
postischemic recirculation of steps
predominantly involved in the initia¬
tion of new peptide chains. Reaggre-
gation of polyribosomes occurred very
slowly when compared to the rapid
recovery of energy metabolism (Fig
4). A normal ribosomal profile
only observed in a monkey, which had
successful recirculation for as long as

drawn from the present series of ex¬
periments: (1) Normothermic nerve
cells can withstand complete ische¬
mia of 60 minutes. (2). Revival of
hindered by extraneural, presumably
In our series of experiments, every
normal in at least a few animals, but
full restoration of all functions in the
same animal did not occur. This rec¬
onciles the different opinions on is¬
chemie brain damage because any

ischemie periods, depending on the
Our method of producing bloodless
ischemia by arterial clamping, with
lowering of the blood pressure and
without obstruction of venous out¬
flow, is apparently superior to pre¬
vious methods in which completeness
of ischemia was achieved either by
more radical procedures, such as sep¬
body,6·26,27 or by techniques which are
complicated by blood stagnation in
the cerebral vessels, by cardiac insuf¬
ficiency, or by both.3"5·24·28"37
It is not likely, however, that com¬
plete neurological recovery will be
obtained with the present technique
unless the problem of impaired post¬
ischemic microcirculation and other
complicating side factors is solved.

Dr. Plum: Thank you, Dr. Hossmann. I
would like to ask one or two simple ques¬
tions of numbers. I think you said that you
had a 50% recovery. Please tell us how
many animals this means with each spe¬
cies: that figure was not mentioned. And,
a 50% revival after 60 minutes of
ischemia strong evidence against
Ames's finding of 90% no-reflow in 15 min¬
utes? My other question is, How late after
recovery was the neuropathologic material
Dr. Hossmann: Fifty percent after 60
minutes is, of course, better than 90% af¬
ter 15 minutes. In most animals that do
not recover, we even know the reason: post¬
restored, there was postischemic hypoten¬
sion, acid base balance was not corrected
We had more than 150 cats and 21 mon¬
keys from which about 55% recovered. The
exact figures of a homogenous group of an¬
imals are given in the paper. Among the
animals that did not recover were those
that had incomplete ischemia. The neuro¬
pathologic material was obtained at differ¬
ent times up to 24 hours after ischemia.
Dr. Brierley asked whether animals, after
60 minutes of circulatory arrest, have re¬
sumed spontaneous respiration? They do,
but it is not desirable to let them breathe
spontaneously because they tend to hyper¬
ventilate. We prefer to immobilize them
again and to have them under artificial
respiration. The second question concerns
my study, together with Dr. Olsson, in
we consistently got morphologic
damage after ischemia of eight minutes.35
This occurred before we began using the
technique of lowering the blood pressure
in order to prevent a collateral blood flow
to the brain. The brain vessels, therefore,
were filled with blood during the initial
period of ischemia, and we got an impair¬
ment of blood recirculation and
quently histologie damage. In the same
study we had another group of animals in
which the blood was removed from the
vessels by perfusing the brain with saline.
In these animals blood recirculation was
not impaired, and histologie damage was
absent after ischemia up to 30 minutes.
Dr. Ames asked how we knew that ische¬
mia was complete. I think I have presented
Dr. Ames further mentions that the rate
of xenon washout would be expected to be
nearly normal in brains in which some of
the vasculature was completely occluded
with the remainder normally patent, de¬
pending on the scale of inhomogeneity.
This is correct when xenon is injected after
the vessels have been occluded. In
experiments xenon was injected into the
brain before the clamping, and
served the absence of clearance. The ques¬

Dr. Plum: Someone asked about the
quality of survival, and I believe you said
the 50% that you're talking about were, in
fact, intact animals. Am I correct? You
said 50% of 150 cats and 20 monkeys sur¬
vived without evidence of deficit. Twenty-
five percent survived but had serious neu¬
rological deficits. Can you clarify this for
us? Contingent on the question of quality
is the duration of survival. In other words,
How long did survival last before you
started these very thoughtful and detailed
Dr. Hossmann: It is important to know
what we call signs of functional recovery;
we do not call it clinical recovery. Signs of
functional recovery are return of sponta¬
neous EEG waves and other neurophysiol¬
ogic signs of function, recovery of energy
metabolism and protein synthesis,
absence of histochemical and morphologic
damage. Recovery starts early after ische¬
mia, but it takes a long time for normali¬
animals in which the EEG was normal,
either in respect to the amplitude or to the
Dr. Plum: We are now approaching one
of the questions that has concerned many
of your readers. How many mammals,
undergoing total cerebral ischemia for 60
minutes in your hands and using your ex¬
perimental measures, can be expected to
recover with brain function adequate for
permanent maintenance of their internal
Dr. Hossmann: I do not know because
this question can only be answered in
long-term experiments. Our method is not
well suited for long-term experiments be¬
we have to perform craniotomy,
tracheotomy, thoracotomy, cannulation of
many arteries, and so forth. We are con¬
cerned with the theoretical question of
whether basic brain functions can return
after 60 minutes, and whatever function
we have investigated up to now has re¬
turned. We do not know if, after 60 min¬
utes of ischemia, the cat will ever catch
Dr. Plum: But how is what you've told
us here different from what we've known
from neuropathologic studies for a long
time: that following anoxic-ischemic inju¬
ry of considerable duration, heroic meas¬
ures can sometimes revive patients and
that the remarkable thing about their
brains is that they are not 100% dead, but
De. Hossmann: If you want to study this
question, first you have to know if, in prin¬
ciple, the nerve cell is able to survive. This
has to be clarified. We now have positive
evidence that any nerve cell in the brain
can survive. We have even seen nerve cells
surviving in the Ammon horn, in the third
layer of the cortex, and in the other "selec-
tively vulnerable" areas. The next ques¬
tion is, Which factors may prevent the re¬
vival of nerve cells? One complication is
the failure of the recirculation,
Fischer has shown. Other complications
are related to the postischemic distur¬
bance of homeostasis of the body fluids. We
have experimental evidence showing that
the faster the homeostasis is restored, the
higher the chance is of revival.
Dr. Ames: I think the fact that there
was such good survival really raises the
question about the impaired reperfusion
after an hour, particularly since the ra¬
dioautograms of the protein synthesis
were so uniform. I find these survival re¬
sults hard for us to argue against. How¬
ever, I think that there is one point worth
considering: As I understand it, the skulls
were open in all these animals. And I am
not at all sure that, as I mentioned before,
having the skull open does not create a
different model. The brain can then com¬
press when you occlude the arteries, and it
is made ischemie with much less blood in
the vessels. We do not know for
against any possible impaired reperfusion
Gary Wise, MD, Columbus, Ohio: Re¬
garding the no-reflow phenomenon and
mortality and the duration of complete
ischemia, in an animal with temporary
bilateral carotid obstruction, you can de¬
crease the blood flow only to the cerebral
hemispheres. This was confirmed by in¬
jecting through the left ventricle colloidal
carbon during bilateral carotid occlusion.
The cerebral hemispheres did not perfuse
in eight of ten animals, while the brain
stem and cerebellum did perfuse. The time
limit in which transient bilateral carotid
occlusion appears to produce microvascu¬
lar obstruction is between 25 and 30 min¬
utes. That is, very few animals have mi¬
crovascular obstruction after 25 minutes,
but 80% of 24 animals had obstruction af¬
ter 30 minutes of carotid occlusion. Put
another way, 80% of the animals will in¬
farct and die within 30 minutes of occlu¬
sion, but only 20% of the animals undergo¬
ing 25-minute occlusions do so. It appears
that the critical time interval without dis¬
turbing the basal motor centers or the
systemic vasculature may be between 25
Dr. Siesjö: Since it has been claimed by
many that barbiturates have a protective
effect, I was wondering if the revival of
biochemical and electrophysiologic func¬
tions could be related to the anesthesia?
Dr. Hossmann: This may be related to
anesthesia, although I do not think so. We
anesthetized cats with chloralose and had
the same results. Barbiturates bring the
energy rich compounds up to a high lev-
el,but the ischemia is so long in our experi¬
ments that there is an almost complete
breakdown of energy rich phosphate com¬
Ian Cameron, Mb ChB, London: First,
may I ask a question and, depending on
the answer, make a brief comment? Dur¬
ing ischemia the sodium:potassium ratio
increased. Am I right in supposing that
total slab of tissue and were therefore wet
weight electrolytes? Second, am I correct
in thinking that you interpreted this as a
loss of potassium into the CSF pool during
Dr. Hossmann: There are actually two
mechanisms. The sodium:potassium ratio
in the white matter is higher than in the
gray matter. During ischemia, the ratio
increases in the gray matter and decreases
in the white matter. So, there is some kind
of equalization within the brain, but there
is evidence that there is also a loss of po¬
Dr. Cameron: Then, if there is a loss
there, may I put forward a hypothesis to
explain the lack of reperfusion? First, I
have two facts: I think that a number of
people have now shown that a moderate
increase in extracellular potassium
centration causes vasodilation. Dr. Betz
has shown that a much higher concentra¬
tion of potassium on the vessels is a con¬
strictor, and this agrees with the findings
in other vascular beds. During ischemia,
the extracellular potassium level rises.
The stage at which you start reperfusion is
crucial because the action of potassium is
biphasic. You begin when the potassium
level is in the dilator range and you can
reperfuse the brain; if you wait until the
potassium level rises to the constrictor
range, however, reperfusion will be limit¬
Thomas Duffy, Phd, New York: Dr.
resynthesis of ATP and higher steady state
levels of lactate in brains of recovered ani¬
mals remind me of the data that Mac-
Ilwain has reported in unstimulated brain
slices, and suggest either decreased cere¬
bral metabolism in those brains, or per¬
an impairment of oxidative phos-
phorylation. Therefore, I wonder if you
were able to assess cerebral metabolic rate
for oxygen in the recovered animals. I
would suspect that it would be decreased.
Dr. Kleihues: Evidence from our data
indicate that incomplete restoration of
ATP levels is indeed a consequence of a
loss of total adenine nueleotides, which are
lost during the ischemie period by dephos-
phorylation of AMP and deamination of
adenosine, leading to the formation of in-
osine, hypoxanthine, and uric acid. A part
of these can be reused for synthesis. De
novo biosynthesis of purines is, unfortu¬
nately, very slow, and although stimulat¬
ed, it may take one or two days to establish
fully the total nucleotide content. If you
have reestablished this content, then you
may hopefully have normal ATP levels.
Point 2: We have worked with slices, espe¬
cially on protein synthesis. We found
vivo, we still had a decreased rate of pro¬
tein synthesis. I think the slice work in
vitro is really not suitable for this kind of

Dr. Kleihues: What we showed was all
in vivo work. There was no ATP at all af¬
ter a few minutes of ischemia; it remained
absent for the rest of the ischemie period
and was rapidly resynthesized during re-
circulation. This indicates a recovery of
cerebral energy metabolism by oxidative
phosphorylation. Other signs of metabolic
reactivation, eg, recovery of protein syn¬
thesis and purine denovo synthesis, were
also present. To estimate brain function,
electrophysiologic recordings, eg, the re¬
turn of spontaneous EEG activity,
more meaningful. You will admit, how¬
ever, that the presence of ATP is a prere¬
quisite for any functional recovery.
